rookie.title: Studio Secrets
rookie.flavor: The golden age of Hollywood turns deadly when a prominent figure is found dead on a bustling film set. Behind the glamorous facade, professional jealousies and career ambitions create a web of suspects with secrets to hide.

items.name: Victoria Sterling
items.detail: Veteran actress whose career is threatened by younger talent.

items.name: Marcus Webb
items.detail: Ambitious director known for his ruthless pursuit of perfection.

items.name: Eleanor Chase
items.detail: Rising starlet who has been stealing roles from established actors.

items.name: Frank Morrison
items.detail: Studio executive with connections to organized crime.

items.name: Stage Sword
items.detail: A prop sword with a real steel blade used in the current production.

items.name: Lighting Rig
items.detail: Heavy equipment that could be sabotaged to fall on someone.

items.name: Camera Tripod
items.detail: Solid metal equipment heavy enough to be used as a bludgeon.

items.name: Poison Capsule
items.detail: A small capsule containing cyanide hidden in prescription medication.

items.name: Dressing Room
items.detail: The victim's private space where he prepared for scenes and stored personal items.

items.name: Sound Stage 7
items.detail: The main filming location buzzing with cast and crew all day.

items.name: Director's Office
items.detail: Private workspace containing scripts and confidential documents.

items.name: Studio Commissary
items.detail: The dining hall where cast and crew gather for meals.

clues.heading: Hidden Medicine
clues.text: A prescription bottle for heart medication is found in the victim's dressing room, with one capsule replaced by a cyanide duplicate. The victim's medical records show no history of heart problems — someone planted it.
clues.alone: Someone with access to the victim's dressing room planted a poisoned capsule.

links.insight: Frank Morrison illegally obtained heart medication from multiple pharmacies. The poison capsule was planted in the dressing room — where only Morrison had unsupervised executive access.

clues.heading: Overheard Argument
clues.text: A grip reports hearing Victoria Sterling and the victim arguing about casting decisions. Later the same grip saw Marcus Webb enter and exit the victim's dressing room alone during the lunch break.
clues.alone: Two suspects had contact with the victim's dressing room area — one had a public fight, one had private access.

links.insight: Victoria's argument was witnessed publicly and she left the lot entirely. Marcus Webb's quiet entry into the dressing room during lunch is the more significant detail.

clues.heading: Fingerprint Evidence
clues.text: Eleanor Chase's fingerprints are found on the victim's coffee cup and nearby script pages. She claims they worked together on line rehearsals that morning.
clues.alone: Eleanor Chase had recent physical contact with items belonging to the victim.

links.insight: Eleanor's fingerprints are fully explained by the scheduled rehearsal. Frank Morrison's fingerprints, however, were found on the dressing room door handle with no legitimate reason to be there.

clues.heading: Pharmacy Records
clues.text: Frank Morrison visited three different pharmacies last week, purchasing heart medication under false pretenses. He claimed it was for his ailing father — but his father died two years ago.
clues.alone: Frank Morrison illegally obtained heart medication with no legitimate explanation.

links.insight: Frank Morrison illegally obtained heart medication from multiple pharmacies. The poison capsule was planted in the dressing room — where only Morrison had unsupervised executive access.

clues.heading: Phone Call Testimony
clues.text: The studio switchboard confirms Victoria Sterling received an urgent call from her agent during lunch and immediately left the lot. Security logs her exit at 12:08 PM — before the time of death.
clues.alone: Victoria Sterling left the studio entirely before the victim died.

links.insight: Victoria's argument was witnessed publicly and she left the lot entirely. Marcus Webb's quiet entry into the dressing room during lunch is the more significant detail.

clues.heading: Rehearsal Schedule
clues.text: The assistant director's logs confirm Eleanor Chase was in a scheduled rehearsal with the victim from 10 AM to noon. Frank Morrison had no scheduled meetings with the victim but was seen near his dressing room at 12:30 PM.
clues.alone: Eleanor Chase's presence is fully accounted for. Frank Morrison's presence near the dressing room is not.

links.insight: Eleanor's fingerprints are fully explained by the scheduled rehearsal. Frank Morrison's fingerprints, however, were found on the dressing room door handle with no legitimate reason to be there.

rookie.epilogue: Frank Morrison's gambling debts to dangerous men had reached a breaking point, and the victim had discovered his embezzlement from studio funds. Faced with exposure and certain death from his creditors, Morrison used his executive pass to slip into the victim's dressing room during the lunch chaos and swap one capsule in the medication bottle. He knew the victim took his pills before every afternoon shoot. The investigation revealed Morrison had been skimming money for months, desperately trying to stay ahead of the organized crime figures who showed no mercy to debtors.

detective.title: Studio Secrets
detective.flavor: The golden age of Hollywood turns deadly when a prominent figure is found dead on a bustling film set. Behind the glamorous facade, professional jealousies and career ambitions create a web of suspects with secrets to hide.

items.name: Victoria Sterling
items.detail: Veteran actress whose career is threatened by younger talent.

items.name: Marcus Webb
items.detail: Ambitious director known for his ruthless pursuit of perfection.

items.name: Eleanor Chase
items.detail: Rising starlet who has been stealing roles from established actors.

items.name: Frank Morrison
items.detail: Studio executive with connections to organized crime.

items.name: Tommy Rodriguez
items.detail: Head of the prop department with access to all studio equipment.

items.name: Stage Sword
items.detail: A prop sword with a real steel blade.

items.name: Lighting Rig
items.detail: Heavy equipment that could be sabotaged.

items.name: Camera Tripod
items.detail: Solid metal, heavy enough to bludgeon.

items.name: Poison Capsule
items.detail: Cyanide hidden in a prescription medication capsule.

items.name: Electrical Cable
items.detail: High-voltage wiring rigged to deliver a fatal shock.

items.name: Dressing Room
items.detail: The victim's private space where he took his medication before afternoon shoots.

items.name: Sound Stage 7
items.detail: The main filming location with full cast and crew present.

items.name: Director's Office
items.detail: Private workspace with scripts and confidential documents.

items.name: Studio Commissary
items.detail: The dining hall where Frank Morrison held his alibi meeting.

items.name: Equipment Storage
items.detail: Warehouse area containing cameras, lights, and technical gear.

items.name: Morning Setup (8-10 AM)
items.detail: Early crew preparation before main cast arrives.

items.name: First Rehearsal (10 AM-12 PM)
items.detail: Initial run-through with principal actors and director.

items.name: Lunch Break (12-1 PM)
items.detail: Cast and crew meal period — dressing room corridor was quiet.

items.name: Afternoon Filming (1-4 PM)
items.detail: Main shooting schedule with full cast and crew present.

items.name: Evening Wrap (4-6 PM)
items.detail: Final shots and equipment breakdown for the day.

clues.heading: Hidden Medicine
clues.text: A prescription bottle in the victim's dressing room has one capsule replaced with a cyanide duplicate. The victim's medical records show no heart condition — someone planted it knowing his afternoon medication routine.
clues.alone: A poisoned capsule was planted in the dressing room by someone who knew the victim's habits.

links.insight: Morrison obtained heart medication illegally with no legitimate explanation. Combined with his executive access to the dressing room corridor, he is the only suspect who had both the means and opportunity to plant the capsule.

clues.heading: Security Guard Report
clues.text: The night security guard saw Marcus Webb leaving the studio at 11 PM the previous evening. Webb claims he was working late reviewing dailies — but the guard noted he came specifically from the direction of the dressing room corridor, not the editing suite.
clues.alone: Marcus Webb was in the dressing room area of the studio at unusual hours.

links.insight: Webb's late-night visit to the dressing room corridor was to review footage he'd found implicating Morrison — he was building a case, not planting evidence. Morrison knew Webb was onto him.

clues.heading: Time of Death
clues.text: Security cameras show the victim entering his dressing room at 12:45 PM, taking medication from the bottle on his vanity, and collapsing within minutes. The narrow time window points to someone active during the lunch break.
clues.alone: The victim died in his dressing room during the lunch break after taking his medication.

links.insight: Morrison was at the commissary during lunch — but security logs show a 12-minute gap where he was unaccounted for. Enough time to slip to the dressing room corridor and back.

clues.heading: Pharmacy Investigation
clues.text: Frank Morrison visited three pharmacies last week obtaining heart medication under false pretenses. His stated reason — his father's prescription — is provably false. His father died two years ago.
clues.alone: Frank Morrison illegally obtained heart medication with no legitimate explanation.

links.insight: Morrison obtained heart medication illegally with no legitimate explanation. Combined with his executive access to the dressing room corridor, he is the only suspect who had both the means and opportunity to plant the capsule.

clues.heading: Film Reel Discovery
clues.text: Marcus Webb's late-night footage review was actually security recordings showing Frank Morrison accessing restricted areas after hours. Webb had compiled the evidence and planned to present it to the studio board that afternoon — the same afternoon the victim was killed.
clues.alone: Webb was gathering evidence against Morrison, not acting suspiciously himself.

links.insight: Webb's late-night visit to the dressing room corridor was to review footage he'd found implicating Morrison — he was building a case, not planting evidence. Morrison knew Webb was onto him.

clues.heading: Commissary Alibi — With a Gap
clues.text: Commissary staff confirm Morrison was present for most of the lunch break in a business meeting. However, a waiter notes he stepped away for approximately 12 minutes just before 12:45 PM, returning slightly out of breath.
clues.alone: Morrison's alibi has a 12-minute gap precisely when the victim was poisoned.

links.insight: Morrison was at the commissary during lunch — but security logs show a 12-minute gap where he was unaccounted for. Enough time to slip to the dressing room corridor and back.

detective.epilogue: Frank Morrison knew Webb had footage of his unauthorized access and was about to expose both his embezzlement and his connection to organized crime. With the victim also closing in on his financial fraud, Morrison spent the previous night planning his move. During a calculated 12-minute break from his alibi lunch meeting, he slipped to the dressing room, swapped the capsule, and returned before anyone noticed. He nearly succeeded — the commissary alibi was almost airtight. Almost.

inspector.title: Studio Secrets
inspector.flavor: The golden age of Hollywood turns deadly when a prominent figure is found dead on a bustling film set. Behind the glamorous facade, professional jealousies and career ambitions create a web of suspects with secrets to hide.

items.name: Victoria Sterling
items.detail: Veteran actress whose career is threatened by younger talent.

items.name: Marcus Webb
items.detail: Ambitious director known for his ruthless pursuit of perfection.

items.name: Eleanor Chase
items.detail: Rising starlet stealing roles from established actors.

items.name: Frank Morrison
items.detail: Studio executive with connections to organized crime.

items.name: Tommy Rodriguez
items.detail: Head of props with access to all studio equipment.

items.name: Dr. Sarah Chen
items.detail: Studio physician who provides medical services to cast and crew.

items.name: Stage Sword
items.detail: A prop sword with a real steel blade.

items.name: Lighting Rig
items.detail: Heavy equipment that could be sabotaged to fall.

items.name: Camera Tripod
items.detail: Solid metal equipment heavy enough to bludgeon.

items.name: Poison Capsule
items.detail: Cyanide hidden in prescription medication.

items.name: Electrical Cable
items.detail: High-voltage wiring rigged for a fatal shock.

items.name: Medical Injection
items.detail: A syringe filled with lethal drugs from the medical office.

items.name: Sound Stage 7
items.detail: The main filming location where the victim was discovered.

items.name: Wardrobe Department
items.detail: Costume storage filled with period clothing.

items.name: Director's Office
items.detail: Private workspace with scripts and confidential documents.

items.name: Studio Commissary
items.detail: The dining hall where cast and crew gather.

items.name: Equipment Storage
items.detail: Warehouse containing cameras, lights, and technical gear.

items.name: Medical Office
items.detail: On-site clinic where cast and crew receive treatment.

items.name: Morning Setup (8-10 AM)
items.detail: Early crew preparation before main cast arrives.

items.name: First Rehearsal (10 AM-12 PM)
items.detail: Initial run-through with principal actors.

items.name: Lunch Break (12-1 PM)
items.detail: Cast and crew meal period when set is mostly empty.

items.name: Afternoon Filming (1-4 PM)
items.detail: Main shooting with full cast and crew.

items.name: Evening Wrap (4-6 PM)
items.detail: Final shots and equipment breakdown.

items.name: Night Security (6 PM-8 AM)
items.detail: After-hours with minimal staff and security patrols.

items.name: Financial Ruin
items.detail: Victim threatened to expose embezzlement leading to prosecution.

items.name: Career Destruction
items.detail: Victim planned to reveal professional misconduct.

items.name: Romantic Betrayal
items.detail: Victim was having an affair that would destroy the killer's marriage.

items.name: Blackmail Scheme
items.detail: Victim discovered illegal activities and demanded payment.

items.name: Professional Rivalry
items.detail: Victim was blocking advancement and stealing creative credit.

items.name: Medical Malpractice
items.detail: Victim knew about falsified credentials and dangerous patient care.

clues.heading: Missing Medical Supplies
clues.text: Dr. Sarah Chen's medical office inventory shows a missing syringe and vial of morphine. The controlled substances log has been altered — a different pen and handwriting replaces the original entries.
clues.alone: Medical supplies capable of causing death have disappeared, and records covering it have been falsified.

links.insight: Tommy Rodriguez purchased medical supplies commercially, but the missing items came from inside the medical office — only Dr. Chen had routine access to those controlled substances.

clues.heading: Assistant Director's Account
clues.text: The assistant director saw Eleanor Chase in deep conversation with the victim near the medical office during lunch break. Both seemed distressed. Eleanor was gesturing toward the medical office door and the victim appeared pale.
clues.alone: Eleanor Chase was having an intense private discussion with the victim near the medical facility at the time of death.

links.insight: Eleanor was urging the victim to report Dr. Chen to the licensing board — she was trying to protect him, not harm him. She had already called the board herself that morning.

clues.heading: Forged Medical Records
clues.text: Hidden in Dr. Chen's office files are forged medical certificates for several cast members, and documentation the victim had signed as a witness on several insurance claims. He had unknowingly authenticated her fraud.
clues.alone: Falsified records bearing the victim's signature are hidden in the medical office — he had unknowingly been used as cover for insurance fraud.

links.insight: The victim's accountant had flagged the insurance anomalies. Chen knew he was close to understanding what he'd signed. Once he did, her fraud — and her falsified medical credentials — would unravel completely.

clues.heading: External Supply Purchases
clues.text: Tommy Rodriguez purchased medical supplies from commercial vendors over the past month for prop purposes — realistic fake injuries for stunt sequences. His receipts and the prop master's sign-off confirm legitimate use.
clues.alone: Tommy Rodriguez's medical supply purchases have a legitimate documented explanation.

links.insight: Tommy Rodriguez purchased medical supplies commercially, but the missing items came from inside the medical office — only Dr. Chen had routine access to those controlled substances.

clues.heading: Phone Records Reveal
clues.text: Eleanor Chase called the medical licensing board the morning of the murder, reporting concerns about Dr. Chen's credentials and treatment practices. The call lasted 15 minutes. She was trying to get the victim to file a formal report.
clues.alone: Eleanor Chase was actively trying to expose Dr. Chen — she was protecting the victim, not threatening him.

links.insight: Eleanor was urging the victim to report Dr. Chen to the licensing board — she was trying to protect him, not harm him. She had already called the board herself that morning.

clues.heading: Financial Investigation
clues.text: Bank records show Dr. Chen receiving large unexplained cash deposits. The victim's accountant confirms he had flagged insurance claims that bore the victim's signature and was preparing a report for the studio board.
clues.alone: Dr. Chen had unexplained income and the victim was about to understand he had unwittingly authenticated her fraud.

links.insight: The victim's accountant had flagged the insurance anomalies. Chen knew he was close to understanding what he'd signed. Once he did, her fraud — and her falsified medical credentials — would unravel completely.

inspector.epilogue: Dr. Sarah Chen had been practicing medicine with falsified credentials for six years, collecting insurance payments for treatments never provided. Eleanor Chase had grown suspicious and was pushing the victim to file a formal report with the licensing board. Chen lured him to the medical office during lunch under the pretense of discussing a health concern, then administered a lethal morphine injection. She intended it to appear as a heart attack. It nearly did. Her desperation to protect a lucrative, dangerous deception cost a man his life — and ultimately cost her everything.
